query: html div vs span interview question

competitors.label: <div>: The Content Division element
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for the div element and the clearest official starting point for generic container behavior and its lack of semantic meaning.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on what div is, where generic containers fit, and why semantic HTML is usually preferable when a meaningful element exists.
theirGaps: The element reference is less interview-shaped and less explicit about the day-to-day production mistake of mixing up block wrappers and inline text-level hooks.

realWorldUseCases: Use <div> and <span> as generic containers only when semantic HTML does not fit. The real pitfall is layout or DOM-hook misuse: <div> changes flow as a block wrapper, while <span> stays inline for text-level targeting.
followUpConfusion: Neither adds semantics, so if you really mean navigation, article content, emphasis, or a button, a semantic element is usually the better choice.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines div clearly, but it stays closer to the reference semantics than to one concrete production decision about block wrappers versus inline hooks.
actionableExamples: It includes canonical examples and semantic guidance, so the example criterion stays competitive rather than flipping fully to us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the follow-up confusion teams actually hit: div and span are not interchangeable, and neither is a substitute for a semantic element.

actionableExamples: Keep the block-versus-inline contrast near the opening so the page stays faster to scan than a full reference entry.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical div reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it translates div-versus-span into the concrete production mistake developers make most: using layout wrappers and text-level hooks interchangeably.
